original_abstract: Using first-principles calculations and deformation potential theory, we investigate the intrinsic carrier mobility ($\mu$) of monolayer $MoS_2$ sheets and nanoribbons. In striking contrast to graphene, which suffers a three-order-of-magnitude drop in mobility upon being patterned into nanoribbons, armchair $MoS_2$ nanoribbons maintain a mobility comparable to their 2D sheet counterpart, despite some oscillation with ribbon width. Surprisingly, we discover a room-temperature transport polarity reversal: while the 2D sheet favors hole transport ($\mu_h = 200.52$ vs. $\mu_e = 72.16$ $cm^2 V^{-1} s^{-1}$), the 4 nm nanoribbon favors electrons ($\mu_e = 190.89$ vs. $\mu_h = 49.72$ $cm^2 V^{-1} s^{-1}$). This robust mobility and the observed polarity flip are driven by the specific symmetry and spatial distribution of edge states inherent in $MoS_2$ nanostructures. Our findings suggest that combining lateral confinement with edge engineering offers a powerful strategy for tailoring and enhancing the transport properties of $MoS_2$-based nanoelectronics.
humanized_abstract: We investigate by means of first-principle calculations within the deformation potential theory the intrinsic carrier mobility of monolayer sheets and nanoribbons of MoS2. Unlike graphene, where the mobility is reduced by almost three orders of magnitude when going from a 2D sheet to nanoribbons, the carrier mobility in armchair nanoribbons of MoS2 is almost the same as that of 2D sheets and barely depends on the width of the ribbon. Moreover, a room temperature transport polarity reversal is observed: the 2D sheet sheet is hole dominant (μh = 200.52 cm2 V−1 s−1 and μe = 72.16 cm2 V−1 s−1) whereas the 4 nm wide nanoribbon is electron dominant (μe = 190.89 cm2 V−1 s−1 and μh = 49.72 cm2 V−1 s−1). The reason for the persistence of the mobility as well as the polarity reversal are related to the particular symmetry and distribution of the edge states present in MoS2 nanostructures. Hence, lateral confinement combined with edge engineering can be an effective way to modulate the transport properties of MoS2 nanoelectronics.

document.output: We investigate by means of first-principle calculations within the deformation potential theory the intrinsic carrier mobility of monolayer sheets and nanoribbons of MoS2. Unlike graphene, where the mobility is reduced by almost three orders of magnitude when going from a 2D sheet to nanoribbons, the carrier mobility in armchair nanoribbons of MoS2 is almost the same as that of 2D sheets and barely depends on the width of the ribbon. Moreover, a room temperature transport polarity reversal is observed: the 2D sheet sheet is hole dominant (μh = 200.52 cm2 V−1 s−1 and μe = 72.16 cm2 V−1 s−1) whereas the 4 nm wide nanoribbon is electron dominant (μe = 190.89 cm2 V−1 s−1 and μh = 49.72 cm2 V−1 s−1). The reason for the persistence of the mobility as well as the polarity reversal are related to the particular symmetry and distribution of the edge states present in MoS2 nanostructures. Hence, lateral confinement combined with edge engineering can be an effective way to modulate the transport properties of MoS2 nanoelectronics.
document.input: Using first-principles calculations and deformation potential theory, we investigate the intrinsic carrier mobility ($\mu$) of monolayer $MoS_2$ sheets and nanoribbons. In striking contrast to graphene, which suffers a three-order-of-magnitude drop in mobility upon being patterned into nanoribbons, armchair $MoS_2$ nanoribbons maintain a mobility comparable to their 2D sheet counterpart, despite some oscillation with ribbon width. Surprisingly, we discover a room-temperature transport polarity reversal: while the 2D sheet favors hole transport ($\mu_h = 200.52$ vs. $\mu_e = 72.16$ $cm^2 V^{-1} s^{-1}$), the 4 nm nanoribbon favors electrons ($\mu_e = 190.89$ vs. $\mu_h = 49.72$ $cm^2 V^{-1} s^{-1}$). This robust mobility and the observed polarity flip are driven by the specific symmetry and spatial distribution of edge states inherent in $MoS_2$ nanostructures. Our findings suggest that combining lateral confinement with edge engineering offers a powerful strategy for tailoring and enhancing the transport properties of $MoS_2$-based nanoelectronics.
